vitals.location: Amsterdam to Arlon, plus an Arlon loop, via Netherlands, Belgium and Luxembourg
vitals.location_badge: Amsterdam to Arlon, Belgium (with Arlon loop)
vitals.entry_fee: 1,000 km solo: EUR 289 waiting list / EUR 309 standard / EUR 359 last-chance, excluding platform fees
vitals.feed_zones: assistance and refreshment points at staffed life-bases
vitals.cutoff_time: 96 hours (1,000 km class), per the 2026 official rules
route_options: 1,000 km (flagship): Amsterdam to Arlon (533.5 km, ~4,700 m) then an Arlon loop (515.3 km, ~7,165 m) — total ~1,048 km / ~11,900 m
500 km, 300 km and 200 km categories also offered per the 2026 official rules

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 73. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 69.

course_description.character: A fixed, organizer-supplied GPX course run by Race Across Series, formerly named 'Race Across Belgium.' For the 2026 season (ran May 27-31, now completed) the organizer renamed and geographically expanded the event: the flagship started at Amsterdam's Olympic Stadium, crossed the flat Dutch and Belgian lowlands to reach Arlon (533.5 km, ~4,700 m), then completed a significantly hillier Arlon loop through the Belgian Ardennes and into Luxembourg (515.3 km, ~7,165 m) — meaning nearly two-thirds of the route's total climbing was concentrated in the second half. This is a semi-autonomous format: riders are self-sufficient between staffed life-bases (food, rest, showers, charging, drop bags), not a fully unsupported race. The organizer's own published route-planning article (issued before the race) carried minor internal inconsistencies (a 1,048 km headline total against a 1,048.8 km leg-sum, and two slightly different figures for the Arlon loop within the same article); these are the organizer's published planning figures, and no post-event public final-GPX audit was found to confirm the exact ridden distance.
course_description.signature_challenge: The Arlon loop through the Belgian Ardennes — roughly half the total distance but nearly two-thirds of the total climbing (~7,165 m of ~11,900 m), arriving after riders had already crossed the flat Netherlands-to-Arlon leg.
suffering_zones: Amsterdam to Arlon opening leg — 533.5 km, ~4,700 m: long, flat Dutch and Belgian lowland miles that front-load fatigue before the climbing begins
The Arlon loop — 515.3 km, ~7,165 m through the Belgian Ardennes and into Luxembourg, the genuinely mountainous heart of the route

Planning-figure inconsistency — the organizer's own pre-race 2026 route article contained internally inconsistent distance/elevation figures; no post-event final-GPX confirmation was found as of this research

logistics.lodging_strategy: Main logistical base in Arlon with rest areas, showers, dormitories, meals.
logistics.transport: Semi-autonomous ultra with staffed life-bases along the route; the 500/300/200 km classes share the Arlon base infrastructure.

history.origin_story: Launched August 19, 2021 in Arlon as 'Race Across Belgium' — the first Belgian ultra-cycling race, initiated by Michel Mussot with 300/600/1,000 km courses. It ran under that name through five editions (2021-2025); the 2025 flagship (Arlon to Braine-l'Alleud) measured approximately 1,015 km / 7,200 m. For the 2026 season, the organizer renamed and geographically expanded the event to 'Race Across Benelux,' extending the route from Belgium-only into a Netherlands-Belgium-Luxembourg crossing. The retired 'Race Across Belgium' name is preserved as a separate historical record at race-across-belgium.json.

biased_opinion.verdict: A Real Belgian Ultra, Renamed and Expanded for 2026 — Not a Gran Fondo
biased_opinion.summary: Race Across Benelux is a genuine, semi-autonomous Race Across Series ultra with five prior editions of organizer track record — it ran as 'Race Across Belgium' from 2021 through 2025 before the organizer renamed and geographically expanded it for 2026, extending the course from a Belgium-only race into a full Netherlands-Belgium-Luxembourg crossing. This is not a mass-participation gran fondo: it's a fixed organizer-GPX ultra with staffed life-bases rather than aid stations, GPS-tracked riders, and a route that front-loads flat Dutch and Belgian lowland miles before a genuinely mountainous Ardennes loop around Arlon. The rename is real and well-documented on the organizer's own site, but it also creates real confusion risk — the event still occupies its old 'race-across-belgique-2025' URL, and legacy calendars and results archives still reference the retired 'Race Across Belgium' name.
strengths: Established organizer track record — five completed editions (2021-2025) under the prior name before this rename, not a first-year unknown
Genuine cross-border route variety: flat Dutch/Belgian lowlands into a real Ardennes mountain loop, rather than a single monotonous terrain type
Real semi-autonomous support infrastructure — staffed life-bases, GPS tracking — more organizer-provided support and monitoring than a pure unsupported race
Multiple distance classes (200/300/500/1,000 km) broaden access beyond the flagship crossing
weaknesses: Brand-new name for 2026 — the organizer's own event page still lives at the old 'race-across-belgique-2025' URL, and results archives still group editions under the retired 'Race Across Belgium' heading, creating real risk of confusing this with a still-active separate Belgium race (there isn't one)

The organizer's own pre-race published 2026 route figures were internally inconsistent — a 1,048 km headline total against a 1,048.8 km leg-sum, and two different figures for the Arlon loop within the same article — a real transparency/precision gap in the planning materials
No post-event public final-GPX audit was found to confirm the exact distance actually ridden versus the pre-race planning figures
No field-size data published as of this research
biased_opinion.bottom_line: A legitimate, five-years-proven Race Across Series ultra that genuinely expanded its ambition for 2026 — worth noting that even the organizer's own pre-race planning materials weren't internally consistent, and don't confuse it with the retired 'Race Across Belgium' name still floating around older calendars.

final_verdict.one_liner: Formerly Race Across Belgium (2021-2025), renamed and expanded for 2026 into a full Amsterdam-to-Arlon-and-back Netherlands/Belgium/Luxembourg crossing — flat lowlands into a real Ardennes climbing loop.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want a proven Race Across Series ultra with real organizer track record — confirm the current year's route and entry details directly with the organizer, since even 2026's own pre-race planning materials weren't internally consistent.
final_verdict.alternatives: For the retired Belgium-only version of this race (2021-2025), see race-across-belgium.json — superseded, same organizer, kept as a historical record only. For a similar Race Across Series format, see Race Across France or Race Across Spain (also in this database).

eligibility.notes: Confirmed SAME EVENT, RENAMED (not a coincidental duplicate): 'Race Across Belgium' ran 2021-2025 (first edition 19 Aug 2021, Arlon start); the organizer renamed and geographically expanded it to 'Race Across Benelux' for the 2026 season (announced Oct-Nov 2025), extending the course into a Netherlands-Belgium-Luxembourg crossing. The retired name is preserved as a historical record at race-across-belgium.json, flagged catalog_flags.duplicate_of pointing here (this profile is canonical). Vitals corrected from a placeholder that exactly duplicated 999.4 km / 11,900 m across both files to the organizer's actual published 2026 figures: 1,048 km / ~11,900 m (Amsterdam to Arlon plus an Arlon loop). The organizer's pre-race route article has internal inconsistencies (1,048 km headline vs 1,048.8 km leg-sum); the 2026 edition ran May 27-31 and is now complete, so course_description language was corrected from prospective/provisional framing to past-tense with a note that no post-event final-GPX audit was found. A follow-up sol adversarial review confirmed the 96h/1,000km cutoff and EUR 289/309/359 solo fee schedule were in fact published (both fields were incorrectly left null/unpublished in the initial draft) and are now populated. Verified 2026-07-24 via codex gpt-5.6-sol foreground research plus a follow-up sol adversarial review, both as part of the Roadie Labs editorial wave 4.

catalog_flags.status_note: fondo_rating.discipline is 'gran_fondo' but this is a fixed-course, semi-autonomous ultra-distance road race with staffed life-bases rather than a mass-start peloton — doesn't cleanly fit the gran_fondo taxonomy, whose scoring dimensions (field_depth, community_culture, etc.) are designed primarily around mass-participation, open-entry events. Recommend a human call on reclassifying discipline or adding scoring caveats, consistent with how flandrien-ride.json flags similar taxonomy mismatches rather than silently changing the discipline field.
